FromTheFounder.title: Leading the Conversation on AI
FromTheFounder.name: Tomas Gorny
FromTheFounder.cardTitle: Don't Wait for AI to Be Perfect. It's Already Doing Work.
FromTheFounder.cardDesc: AI learns through action, not hesitation. The companies seeing real results started imperfect and improved along the way.
FromTheFounder.linkedInPost: There's a false expectation that AI should be flawless out of the gate. That it can go from 0-60 in 0.001 seconds. The truth is, AI needs context. Just like teaching a friend a new skill, you start small, test, and adjust. You build that context over time. Mistakes will happen and they're part of how the system gets smarter. The businesses that will win with AI aren't waiting for perfection…

foodicsReview.metaTitle: Foodics Review 2026: Pricing, Features, ZATCA Compliance & Honest Verdict
foodicsReview.metaDescription: In-depth Foodics review for 2026 covering POS features, inventory management, delivery integration, ZATCA Phase 2 compliance, pricing plans, and how it compares to Square, POSRocket, and iiko.
foodicsReview.heroImageAlt: Foodics POS system displayed on an iPad in a modern restaurant setting with staff processing orders
foodicsReview.heroTitle: Foodics Review 2026: The All-in-One Restaurant POS Built for Saudi Arabia
foodicsReview.authorMeta: Restaurant Tech Analyst at Mansati | Published April 3, 2026
foodicsReview.shareText: Foodics Review 2026: The All-in-One Restaurant POS Built for Saudi Arabia
foodicsReview.keyTakeaway1: Foodics is the most feature-complete restaurant POS platform built specifically for the MENA region, now operating in 35+ countries.
foodicsReview.keyTakeaway2: ZATCA Phase 2 e-invoicing is fully built in -- no third-party plugins or manual workarounds required.
foodicsReview.keyTakeaway3: Pricing starts at SAR 392/month (QSR Starter) but scales quickly once you add branches, delivery, and payroll modules.
foodicsReview.keyTakeaway4: Delivery integration with HungerStation, Jahez, and Marsool works through FeedUs and Blend middleware with automatic stock depletion.
foodicsReview.keyTakeaway5: The platform is iPad-only for the cashier app -- Android and Windows users will need to look elsewhere or invest in Apple hardware.
foodicsReview.s2Title: Foodics Overview: From Riyadh Startup to Regional Powerhouse
foodicsReview.s2Lead: Foodics has grown from a scrappy Riyadh POS startup into the largest cloud restaurant platform in the Middle East. But does the product actually live up to the hype? We spent four weeks testing it.
foodicsReview.s2P1: Founded in 2014, Foodics started as a cloud-based point-of-sale system designed specifically for the Saudi food and beverage market. A decade later, the platform has expanded into a full-stack restaurant operating system covering POS, inventory, kitchen display, CRM, delivery aggregation, HR, payroll, and even embedded lending through Foodics Capital.
foodicsReview.s2P2: The numbers are hard to argue with. Over 30,000 establishments across 35+ countries run on Foodics. The company raised a $170 million Series C in 2022 -- the largest SaaS Series C in MENA history -- led by Prosus and Sanabil Investments. In H1 2025, gross merchandise volume hit $6 billion, up 27% year-over-year.
foodicsReview.s2P3: But big funding rounds and impressive GMV figures do not automatically translate into a great product for the restaurant owner on the ground. This review examines what Foodics actually does, how well it does it, what it costs, and where it falls short.
foodicsReview.s3Intro: Foodics markets itself as an all-in-one platform, and the feature list backs that up. Here is what the platform covers across its core modules.
foodicsReview.s3ImageAlt: Foodics homepage showing the restaurant management platform interface with POS, inventory, and analytics modules
foodicsReview.s3ImageCaption: The Foodics homepage highlights its all-in-one approach to restaurant management.
foodicsReview.s3Feature_pos_desc: iPad-based cashier app with offline mode, table management, split bills, QR ordering, and waiter-side ordering on mobile devices.
foodicsReview.s3Feature_inventory_desc: Real-time stock tracking with recipe-level depletion, low-stock alerts, purchase orders, and multi-location transfer management.
foodicsReview.s3Feature_kds_desc: Digital order routing to kitchen stations, priority queuing, prep time tracking, and bump-bar support for high-volume kitchens.
foodicsReview.s3Feature_crm_desc: Built-in loyalty programs, customer purchase history, segmented marketing, and feedback collection tied directly to transaction data.
foodicsReview.s3Feature_delivery_desc: Native integrations with HungerStation, Jahez, Marsool, and Talabat through FeedUs and Blend middleware. Auto menu sync and stock depletion.
foodicsReview.s3Feature_hr_desc: Employee scheduling, attendance tracking via POS clock-in, payroll calculations with GOSI compliance, and labor cost reporting per branch.
foodicsReview.s3P1: The breadth is genuinely impressive. Most competitors in the MENA region offer two or three of these modules and rely on third-party integrations for the rest. Foodics builds (or acquires) nearly everything in-house.
foodicsReview.s4Title: POS & Cashier App: The iPad-First Experience
foodicsReview.s4Intro: The cashier interface is where staff spend most of their time, so it needs to be fast, intuitive, and reliable. Foodics delivers on two out of three -- with one significant caveat.
foodicsReview.s4P1: The POS runs exclusively on iPads. There is no Android version, no Windows client, and no browser-based fallback. If your restaurant uses Android tablets or Windows terminals, Foodics is not an option unless you invest in Apple hardware. For a deeper look at the Foodics ecosystem and its strategic direction, see our Foodics super-platform analysis.
foodicsReview.s4Feature1: Offline mode that caches transactions locally and syncs automatically when connectivity returns -- critical for Saudi malls where WiFi can be unreliable.
foodicsReview.s4Feature2: Waiter app on iPhone for tableside ordering, reducing trips to the main POS terminal and speeding up service during peak hours.
foodicsReview.s4Feature3: QR code ordering that lets customers scan, browse the menu, and place orders directly from their table without downloading an app.
foodicsReview.s4Feature4: Pay-at-table functionality with integrated payment terminals, eliminating the need for customers to queue at the cashier.
foodicsReview.s4Feature5: Multi-language interface supporting Arabic, English, French, and Turkish -- essential for restaurants with diverse staff in the Gulf region.
foodicsReview.s4P2: In our testing, the POS interface was responsive and well-organized. Menu navigation is logical, modifier handling is smooth, and the split-bill feature works without friction. The offline mode held up during a simulated connectivity drop, with all cached transactions syncing correctly once WiFi returned.
foodicsReview.s4Callout: The iPad-only requirement is Foodics' biggest hardware limitation. A single iPad starts at SAR 1,499 in Saudi Arabia. For a multi-terminal restaurant, the hardware cost adds up quickly before you even pay for the software subscription.
foodicsReview.s5Title: Inventory Management: Real-Time Stock Control Across Locations
foodicsReview.s5Intro: Inventory is where many restaurant POS systems fall apart. Foodics treats it as a first-class feature rather than an afterthought.
foodicsReview.s5Feature1: Real-time stock tracking that updates the moment a sale is processed at the POS, not at end-of-day reconciliation.
foodicsReview.s5Feature2: Recipe-level depletion: when a burger sells, the system automatically deducts the bun, patty, lettuce, and sauce from raw inventory based on predefined recipes.
foodicsReview.s5Feature3: Low-stock alerts with configurable thresholds per ingredient, per location. Alerts can trigger automatic purchase orders to suppliers.
foodicsReview.s5Feature4: Multi-location stock transfers with approval workflows, so a central kitchen can push ingredients to branches based on demand forecasts.
foodicsReview.s5P1: For restaurants managing complex supply chains, this level of granularity is valuable. Our restaurant inventory management guide covers broader options, but Foodics is one of the few platforms where inventory is deeply integrated with the POS rather than bolted on.
foodicsReview.s5P2: The one weakness is reporting. While the raw data is comprehensive, the built-in inventory reports lack the visual depth you get from dedicated inventory tools. Power users will likely export data to spreadsheets for deeper analysis.
foodicsReview.s6Title: Delivery Integration: HungerStation, Jahez, Marsool & More
foodicsReview.s6Intro: Delivery is no longer optional for Saudi restaurants. Foodics connects to the major Saudi delivery platforms through middleware layers rather than direct API integrations.
foodicsReview.s6P1: The integration works through FeedUs and Blend, two middleware platforms that sit between Foodics and the delivery apps. This means orders from HungerStation, Jahez, Marsool, and Talabat flow directly into the Foodics POS without manual re-entry. For context on how delivery platforms are reshaping the Saudi F&B market, see our related analyses.
foodicsReview.s6P2: The middleware approach has a tradeoff: it adds a dependency layer. If FeedUs or Blend experience downtime, delivery orders stop flowing into the POS even though the delivery platforms themselves are still operational. In practice, we experienced no downtime during our testing period, but it is a risk worth noting.
foodicsReview.s6P3: Menu sync is the standout feature. When you update a price or mark an item out of stock in Foodics, the change propagates to all connected delivery platforms within minutes. This eliminates the painful process of logging into each delivery dashboard separately to update menus.
foodicsReview.s7Title: ZATCA Phase 2 Compliance: E-Invoicing Without the Headache
foodicsReview.s7Intro: For Saudi-based restaurants, ZATCA compliance is not optional -- it is a legal requirement. Foodics has invested heavily in making Phase 2 e-invoicing as painless as possible.
foodicsReview.s7ImageAlt: Foodics interface showing business type selection for restaurants, cafes, and food trucks with ZATCA compliance indicators
foodicsReview.s7ImageCaption: Foodics supports multiple business types with built-in ZATCA Phase 2 compliance for each.
foodicsReview.s7Feature1: Phase 2 cryptographic stamps (QR codes) embedded in every invoice, generated automatically at the point of sale without manual intervention.
foodicsReview.s7Feature2: Universally Unique Identifier (UUID) assigned to each invoice for tamper-proof audit trails as required by ZATCA regulations.
foodicsReview.s7Feature3: Real-time reporting to ZATCA servers with automatic retry logic if the initial transmission fails due to connectivity issues.
foodicsReview.s7Feature4: InvoiceQ integration for B2B invoicing scenarios, supporting the XML format required for business-to-business transactions under ZATCA Phase 2.
foodicsReview.s7P1: This is one area where Foodics has a clear advantage over international competitors. Platforms like Square or Toast have no ZATCA support at all -- Saudi restaurants using those systems need expensive third-party plugins or manual workarounds. For businesses also evaluating ERP-level compliance, our Odoo ZATCA compliance guide covers the broader landscape.
foodicsReview.s7Callout: ZATCA Phase 2 enforcement is actively expanding across taxpayer waves. Restaurants that are not compliant risk penalties starting at SAR 5,000 per non-compliant invoice. Foodics generates compliant invoices by default -- no configuration required beyond the initial ZATCA registration.
foodicsReview.s8Title: Pricing Plans: What Foodics Actually Costs in 2026
foodicsReview.s8Intro: Foodics uses a tiered subscription model billed annually. The published pricing covers the software license per cashier device -- hardware, delivery integration add-ons, and additional modules cost extra.
foodicsReview.s8ImageAlt: Foodics pricing page showing the three subscription tiers with monthly rates in Saudi Riyals
foodicsReview.s8ImageCaption: Foodics pricing tiers as of Q1 2026. All prices are per device, billed annually.
foodicsReview.s8Row_basic_features:
Everything in Starter + KDS,
CRM, table management, reporting
foodicsReview.s8Row_advanced_features:
Everything in Basic + multi-location,
HR/payroll, advanced analytics, API access
foodicsReview.s8P1: These are per-device prices on annual billing. A three-terminal restaurant on the Advanced plan pays SAR 3,399 per month in software alone -- before adding delivery integration (SAR 200-400/mo per platform) and hardware costs.
foodicsReview.s8P2: The pricing is competitive for what you get, but it scales up fast. For a detailed look at how POS subscription fees compound over time, see our analysis on POS subscription costs. Foodics does not publish discounts for multi-year commitments, though enterprise customers report negotiating 15-20% off published rates.
foodicsReview.s9Title: Pros and Cons: The Honest Breakdown
foodicsReview.s9Pro1: All-in-one platform eliminates the need for 5-7 separate vendor relationships. POS, inventory, KDS, CRM, delivery, and HR in a single login.
foodicsReview.s9Pro2: Deep MENA localization with Arabic-first interface, SAR pricing, bilingual receipt generation, and Gulf-specific tax configurations.
foodicsReview.s9Pro3: ZATCA Phase 2 compliance built into every invoice by default -- no plugins, no manual configuration, no third-party dependencies.
foodicsReview.s9Pro4: Delivery API integrations with HungerStation, Jahez, and Marsool through FeedUs/Blend, including automatic menu sync and stock depletion.
foodicsReview.s9Pro5: Robust offline mode that caches transactions locally and syncs reliably when connectivity returns, tested in our four-week evaluation.
foodicsReview.s9Con1: iPad-only POS client. No Android, no Windows, no browser-based option. This locks you into Apple hardware costs that can exceed SAR 6,000 for a multi-terminal setup.
foodicsReview.s9Con2: Pricing scales aggressively. A three-branch restaurant with delivery integration can easily exceed SAR 5,000/month in software fees alone.
foodicsReview.s9Con3: Complex initial setup. Multi-location inventory rules, recipe definitions, and delivery platform connections require dedicated onboarding time -- Foodics estimates 2-4 weeks for full deployment.
foodicsReview.s9P1: The pros heavily outweigh the cons for most Saudi restaurant operators. The iPad limitation is the most frustrating constraint because it is a deliberate product decision, not a technical limitation. Foodics has the engineering resources to build an Android client -- they have chosen not to.
foodicsReview.s9Cta: See If Foodics Fits Your Restaurant
foodicsReview.s10Intro: Foodics is not for everyone. It is overbuilt for a single food truck and overpriced for a home bakery. But for specific operator profiles, it is the strongest option in the MENA market.
foodicsReview.s10Card_franchise_desc: Centralized inventory, unified reporting across locations, and branch-level P&L analysis make Foodics the default choice for franchise operations with 3+ locations in Saudi Arabia.
foodicsReview.s10Card_delivery_desc: If 40%+ of your revenue comes from delivery platforms, the automated order flow and stock depletion across HungerStation, Jahez, and Marsool justifies the subscription cost.
foodicsReview.s10Card_datadriven_desc: Operators who want real-time sales analytics, labor cost tracking, and ingredient-level food cost reporting will find Foodics' data layer significantly deeper than competitors.
foodicsReview.s10Card_zatca_desc: Businesses that need guaranteed Phase 2 compliance without third-party dependencies or manual workarounds. Foodics generates compliant invoices by default from day one.
foodicsReview.s10P1: If you are a single-location cafe doing under SAR 30,000 in monthly revenue, Foodics is likely more platform than you need. A simpler POS like Loyverse or Square (where available) would serve you at a fraction of the cost.
foodicsReview.s11Title: Foodics vs Square vs POSRocket vs iiko: How They Compare
foodicsReview.s11Intro: Choosing a restaurant POS in Saudi Arabia comes down to a handful of realistic options. Here is how Foodics stacks up against the most relevant competitors. For broader CRM and ERP comparisons, see our related guides.
foodicsReview.s11P1: Square offers the lowest entry cost but lacks ZATCA compliance and deep Saudi delivery integrations. POSRocket was a strong local competitor until Foodics acquired it in 2023 -- it continues to operate independently but product development has slowed. iiko targets enterprise-scale operations with complex kitchen workflows but requires significant customization and lacks native MENA localization.
foodicsReview.s11P2: For Saudi restaurants that need ZATCA compliance, delivery integration, and Arabic-first design, Foodics remains the most complete option. The iPad-only constraint and higher pricing are the main reasons operators look elsewhere.
foodicsReview.s12Q1: Does Foodics integrate with HungerStation and Jahez for delivery orders?
foodicsReview.s12A1: Yes. Foodics connects to HungerStation and Jahez through FeedUs middleware, and to Marsool and Talabat through Blend middleware. Orders flow directly into the POS with automatic menu sync and stock depletion. There is no need to re-enter delivery orders manually.
foodicsReview.s12A2: Yes. Foodics generates ZATCA Phase 2 compliant invoices by default, including cryptographic stamps, UUID assignment, and real-time reporting to ZATCA servers. B2B invoicing is supported through InvoiceQ integration. No third-party plugins are required.
foodicsReview.s12Q3: Does Foodics work on Android tablets?
foodicsReview.s12A3: No. The Foodics cashier app runs exclusively on iPads. There is no Android, Windows, or browser-based version. The waiter app runs on iPhones. If your restaurant uses Android hardware, you will need to either switch to iPads or consider alternatives like POSRocket or Square.
foodicsReview.s12Q4: How much does Foodics cost for a multi-branch restaurant?
foodicsReview.s12A4: Pricing depends on the plan and number of devices. A three-terminal restaurant on the Advanced plan pays approximately SAR 3,399/month in software fees. Add delivery integration (SAR 200-400/month per platform) and iPad hardware costs. Enterprise customers can negotiate 15-20% off published rates.
foodicsReview.s12Q5: Does Foodics include HR and payroll features?
foodicsReview.s12A5: Yes, on the Advanced plan. Features include employee scheduling, POS-based clock-in/clock-out, payroll calculations with GOSI compliance, and labor cost reporting per branch. The Starter and Basic plans do not include HR/payroll modules.
foodicsReview.s13P1: Foodics earns a 4.5 out of 5 as the most complete restaurant management platform available in Saudi Arabia and the broader MENA region. The all-in-one approach eliminates vendor sprawl, ZATCA compliance is seamless, and delivery integrations work as advertised.
foodicsReview.s13P2: The half-point deduction comes from two persistent issues: the iPad-only hardware requirement adds unnecessary cost for operators who already own Android or Windows terminals, and the pricing scales aggressively for multi-branch operations. These are not dealbreakers, but they are real costs that affect the total value proposition.
foodicsReview.s13P3: For Saudi restaurants doing SAR 50,000+ in monthly revenue with delivery exposure and multi-location ambitions, Foodics is our recommended choice. The platform depth, ZATCA compliance, and delivery integrations create a combination that no single competitor currently matches in the MENA market.

howToBuildShopifyStore.metaTitle: How to Build a Shopify Store in Saudi Arabia (2026 Step-by-Step Guide)
howToBuildShopifyStore.metaDescription: Complete 10-step guide to launching a Shopify store in Saudi Arabia — from sign-up to ZATCA compliance, Tap Payments, Arabic RTL setup, and launch checklist.
howToBuildShopifyStore.heroTitle: How to Build a Shopify Store in Saudi Arabia
howToBuildShopifyStore.heroImageAlt: Shopify store setup guide for Saudi Arabia with dashboard screenshot
howToBuildShopifyStore.authorMeta: We test e-commerce platforms hands-on so you can launch with confidence.
howToBuildShopifyStore.keyTakeaway1: Shopify offers a 3-day free trial followed by $1/month for the first 3 months — no credit card required to start.
howToBuildShopifyStore.keyTakeaway2: Shopify Payments is NOT available in Saudi Arabia — use Tap Payments to accept Mada, STC Pay, and Apple Pay.
howToBuildShopifyStore.keyTakeaway3: You must configure 15% VAT (ZATCA-compliant) and use an app like Marmin for e-invoicing.
howToBuildShopifyStore.keyTakeaway4: Aramex and SMSA shipping can be integrated via the OTO app for domestic delivery.
howToBuildShopifyStore.keyTakeaway5: Always test your store with a test order, verify RTL Arabic layout, and confirm SSL before going live.
howToBuildShopifyStore.shareText: How to Build a Shopify Store in Saudi Arabia — the complete 2026 guide by Mansati
howToBuildShopifyStore.s2Title: Why Shopify for E-Commerce in Saudi Arabia?
howToBuildShopifyStore.s2Intro: Saudi Arabia's e-commerce market is projected to exceed $13 billion by 2027. Shopify powers over 4.8 million stores worldwide and is the most popular hosted e-commerce platform for a reason: it handles hosting, security, and PCI compliance so you can focus on selling.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s2P1: For Saudi sellers, Shopify offers Arabic language support (via the Translate & Adapt app), multiple currency options including SAR, and a growing ecosystem of apps built for the MENA market. The platform scales from a single-product store to a multi-million SAR operation without switching systems.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s2P2: However, Shopify Payments is not yet available in Saudi Arabia, which means you will need a third-party payment gateway like Tap Payments or Moyasar. This guide walks you through every step — including the Saudi-specific configurations most tutorials skip.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s3Intro: Getting started takes less than 5 minutes. Shopify currently offers a 3-day free trial, then just $1/month for the first 3 months — no credit card required to begin.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s3Step1: Go to shopify.com and click "Start free trial."
howToBuildShopifyStore.s3Step2: Enter your email address, create a password, and choose a store name (this becomes your-store.myshopify.com — you can add a custom domain later).
howToBuildShopifyStore.s3Step3: Answer the onboarding questions: what you plan to sell, whether you are already selling, and your current revenue. These help Shopify personalize your dashboard but do not lock you into anything.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s3Step4: Select your country as Saudi Arabia. This sets your default currency to SAR and applies the correct tax region.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s3Step5: You will land on your Shopify admin dashboard. No credit card is needed until the free trial ends.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s3Tip: Pro Tip: Use a business email (not personal Gmail) when signing up. It looks more professional on order confirmations and helps if you need to contact Shopify support later.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s4Intro: Before customizing anything, take 10 minutes to explore the admin. Understanding the layout saves hours later.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s4Step1: The left sidebar is your command center: Home, Orders, Products, Customers, Content, Analytics, Marketing, Discounts, and Settings.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s4Step2: Home shows your setup guide checklist — Shopify tracks your progress and prompts next steps.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s4Step3: Orders is where all incoming purchases appear. You can fulfill, refund, and archive orders here.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s4Step4: Products is where you add items, manage inventory, set prices, and organize collections.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s4Step5: Settings (bottom-left) is the most important section for Saudi sellers — this is where you configure payments, shipping, taxes, domains, and legal policies.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s4Tip: Pro Tip: Bookmark your admin URL (your-store.myshopify.com/admin). The Shopify mobile app also gives you full admin access on the go.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s5Intro: Your theme controls how your store looks. Shopify's Theme Store has 200+ themes (12 free, rest paid $180–$400). For new stores, we recommend starting with Dawn — Shopify's default free theme.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s5Step1: Go to Online Store → Themes in your admin sidebar.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s5Step2: Click "Explore free themes" or visit the Shopify Theme Store for premium options.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s5Step3: Preview themes before installing. Look for: mobile responsiveness, page load speed, and built-in sections that match your product type.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s5Step4: Click "Customize" to open the visual drag-and-drop editor. You can change colors, fonts, layout sections, and add content blocks without writing code.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s5Step5: For Arabic RTL support, install the Translate & Adapt app and add this CSS snippet to your theme's custom CSS: html[lang="ar"] '{' direction: rtl; text-align: right; '}'
howToBuildShopifyStore.s5Tip: Pro Tip: Do not buy a premium theme until you have at least 50 orders. Dawn is fast, clean, and fully featured. You can always switch themes later without losing products or orders.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s6Intro: Products are the core of your store. Shopify supports physical goods, digital downloads, services, and gift cards.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s6Step1: In your Shopify admin, go to Products → Add product.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s6Step2: Upload product images — use high-quality photos (minimum 2048x2048 pixels). Shopify auto-compresses and serves WebP format. Add at least 3–5 images per product.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s6Step3: Set inventory tracking: enter SKU, barcode (if applicable), and available quantity. Enable "Track quantity" to get low-stock alerts.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s6Step4: Add variants if your product comes in different sizes, colors, or materials. Each variant can have its own price, SKU, and inventory count.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s6Step5: Scroll to the SEO section at the bottom. Edit the page title and meta description — include your main keyword and keep the title under 60 characters.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s6Step6: Organize products into Collections (e.g., "New Arrivals," "Best Sellers," "Abayas"). Collections can be manual or automated based on tags, price, or vendor.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s6Tip: Pro Tip: Write product descriptions that answer the top 3 questions a buyer would ask. Include dimensions, materials, care instructions, and shipping time for Saudi customers.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s7Intro: This is the most critical Saudi-specific step. Shopify Payments is NOT available in Saudi Arabia, so you must use a third-party payment provider.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s7Step1: Go to Settings → Payments in your Shopify admin.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s7Step2: Under "Payment providers," click "Choose a provider" and search for Tap Payments — the most widely used gateway in Saudi Arabia.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s7Step3: Search for and install Tap Payments — the leading payment gateway for Saudi Arabia supporting Mada, STC Pay, and Apple Pay.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s7Step4: Connect Tap Payments to Shopify by entering your API keys (found in your Tap dashboard under Developers → API Keys).
howToBuildShopifyStore.s7Step5: Tap Payments supports: Mada debit cards, Visa/Mastercard, Apple Pay, STC Pay, and Tabby (buy now pay later). Fees are typically 2.5% + 1 SAR per transaction.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s7Step6: Enable Cash on Delivery (COD) under "Manual payment methods" — still very popular in Saudi Arabia, accounting for roughly 30% of e-commerce transactions.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s7Tip: Shopify Payments is not available in Saudi Arabia. You must use a third-party gateway like Tap Payments, PayTabs, or HyperPay. This adds a 2.0% fee on Basic, 1.0% on Grow, or 0.6% on Advanced — on top of the gateway's own fees.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s8Intro: Saudi customers expect fast, affordable delivery. Shopify lets you set flat rates, weight-based rates, or real-time carrier rates.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s8Step1: Go to Settings → Shipping and delivery in your Shopify admin.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s8Step2: Create a shipping zone for Saudi Arabia. You can segment by region (Riyadh, Jeddah, Eastern Province, etc.) with different rates.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s8Step3: Set your rates: flat rate (e.g., 25 SAR nationwide), free shipping over a threshold (e.g., free over 200 SAR), or weight-based pricing.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s8Step4: For carrier integration, install the OTO app from the Shopify App Store. OTO connects you to Aramex, SMSA, DHL, and J&T Express with auto-generated waybills and tracking.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s8Step5: For automated shipping, install the OTO app from the Shopify App Store — it connects Aramex, SMSA, SPL, and J&T Express in one integration.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s8Tip: Pro Tip: Offer free shipping on orders over 200 SAR. Data shows this increases average order value by 15–25% in the Saudi market.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s9Intro: All businesses selling in Saudi Arabia must charge 15% VAT and comply with ZATCA (Zakat, Tax and Customs Authority) e-invoicing regulations.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s9Step1: Go to Settings → Taxes and duties in your Shopify admin.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s9Step2: Click on Saudi Arabia under your tax regions. Set the VAT rate to 15%.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s9Step3: Enable "Include tax in prices" — Saudi consumers expect to see the final price including VAT, not a price-plus-tax model.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s9Step4: Register for a VAT number with ZATCA at zatca.gov.sa if your annual revenue exceeds 375,000 SAR (mandatory) or 187,500 SAR (voluntary registration).
howToBuildShopifyStore.s9Step5: For ZATCA Phase 2 e-invoicing compliance, install the Marmin app — it generates compliant XML invoices with QR codes that integrate directly with ZATCA's Fatoorah platform.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s9Tip: Pro Tip: Even if you are below the mandatory VAT threshold, register voluntarily. It adds credibility, allows you to reclaim input VAT, and avoids a rush when you cross the threshold.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s10Intro: A custom domain (e.g., your-brand.com.sa) looks professional and builds trust. You can buy through Shopify or connect an existing domain.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s10Step1: Go to Settings → Domains in your Shopify admin.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s10Step2: If buying new: click "Buy new domain" — Shopify domains start at $14/year for .com. For .com.sa, you will need to register through a Saudi registrar like Saudi NIC.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s10Step3: If connecting an existing domain: click "Connect existing domain" and enter your domain name.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s10Step4: In your Shopify admin, go to Settings → Domains → Connect existing domain. Enter your domain and click Connect.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s10Step5: Wait for DNS propagation (usually 1–48 hours). Shopify automatically provisions a free SSL certificate once the domain is verified.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s10Tip: Pro Tip: Always use a .com or .com.sa domain for Saudi stores. Avoid obscure TLDs — they hurt trust and can lower ad click-through rates.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s11Intro: Shopify's App Store has 13,000+ apps. For a Saudi store, these five are essential from day one:
howToBuildShopifyStore.s11Step1: Install Marmin: ZATCA Invoice with QR — mandatory for Saudi e-invoicing compliance. It auto-generates QR-coded invoices that sync with ZATCA Phase 2.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s11Step2: Install OTO — multi-carrier shipping aggregator connecting Aramex, SMSA, SPL, and J&T Express with one-click label generation.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s11Step3: Install Tap Payments — enables Mada, STC Pay, and Apple Pay at checkout for Saudi customers.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s11Step4: Install Shopify Translate & Adapt — free app for bilingual Arabic/English storefronts with side-by-side editing.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s11Step5: Install Sufio — beautifully designed ZATCA-compliant PDF invoices with automatic VAT calculations.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s11Tip: Pro Tip: Do not install more than 8–10 apps total. Each app adds JavaScript that slows your store. Remove any app you are not actively using.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Intro: Before removing your store password and going live, run through this checklist:
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Step1: Place a test order using your payment gateway's test mode. Verify it appears in Orders in your Shopify admin.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Step2: Verify ZATCA invoice: if using Marmin, confirm the test order generates a compliant e-invoice with a valid QR code.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Step3: Check RTL Arabic layout: switch your store to Arabic and browse every page. Look for text alignment issues, overlapping elements, and broken navigation.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Step4: Confirm SSL is active: your URL should show a padlock icon. Shopify provides free SSL but it only activates after your custom domain is verified.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Step5: Review legal pages: ensure you have Privacy Policy, Terms of Service, Refund Policy, and Shipping Policy. Shopify can auto-generate templates under Settings → Policies.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Step6: Test mobile experience: over 78% of Saudi e-commerce traffic comes from mobile. Check product pages, checkout flow, and payment on both iOS and Android.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Step7: Set up Google Analytics 4 and the Facebook Pixel for tracking. Go to Online Store → Preferences to add your tracking codes.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s12Tip: Pro Tip: Share your store URL with 5–10 trusted people before the public launch. Fresh eyes catch issues you have been staring past for days.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Intro: After your 3-day free trial and 90-day $1/month period, you'll need to choose a plan. Visit Shopify Pricing for the latest details.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Plan1Features: 2 staff accounts, up to 4 inventory locations, 2.0% third-party transaction fee, basic reports, 77% shipping discount
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Plan2Features: 5 staff accounts, up to 5 inventory locations, 1.0% third-party transaction fee, professional reports, 88% shipping discount
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Plan3Features: 15 staff accounts, up to 8 inventory locations, 0.6% third-party transaction fee, custom reports, 88% shipping discount, duties & import tax calculation
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Plan4Features: Unlimited staff accounts, 200 inventory locations, 0.2% third-party transaction fee, Shopify Flow automation, checkout customization with Checkout Extensibility, dedicated support
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13P1: For most Saudi stores starting out, the Basic plan at $39/month is sufficient. Upgrade to Grow when the 1% lower transaction fee savings exceed the $66/month price difference — that happens around $6,600/month in revenue through third-party gateways.
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq1Q: Can I use Shopify in Saudi Arabia?
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq1A: Yes. Shopify offers a 3-day free trial with no credit card required. After that, you pay just $1/month for the first 3 months.
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq2Q: How much does Shopify cost in Saudi Arabia?
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq2A: Shopify starts with a 3-day free trial, then $1/month for 3 months. After that, the Basic plan is $39/month (approximately 146 SAR). You will also pay transaction fees to your payment gateway (typically 2.5% + 1 SAR per transaction with Tap Payments) plus the 2.0% Shopify third-party fee on the Basic plan.
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq3Q: Does Shopify support Arabic and RTL?
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq3A: Shopify supports Arabic through the free Translate & Adapt app. Most modern themes (including Dawn) support RTL layout. You may need to add a small CSS override for perfect RTL rendering: html[lang='ar'] '{' direction: rtl; text-align: right; '}'
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq4Q: What payment methods can Saudi customers use?
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq4A: Through Tap Payments, your customers can pay with Mada debit cards, Visa, Mastercard, Apple Pay, STC Pay, and Tabby (buy now pay later). You should also enable Cash on Delivery, which still accounts for about 30% of Saudi e-commerce transactions.
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq5Q: Do I need a commercial registration to sell on Shopify in Saudi Arabia?
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq5A: To accept payments through Tap Payments or any Saudi payment gateway, yes — you need a valid Commercial Registration (CR) from the Ministry of Commerce. You will also need a Saudi bank account (IBAN) and a valid Saudi ID or Iqama.
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq6Q: How do I handle shipping in Saudi Arabia?
howToBuildShopifyStore.faq6A: Install the OTO app to connect with Aramex, SMSA, DHL, and J&T Express. OTO auto-generates waybills and tracking pages. Most Saudi stores offer flat-rate shipping (20–30 SAR) with free shipping over 200 SAR.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s15Title: Final Tips for Saudi Shopify Sellers
howToBuildShopifyStore.s15P1: Start with the Basic plan and the Dawn theme. Do not overspend on premium themes or apps before you have validated your product-market fit. Your first 100 orders will teach you more than any planning session.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s15P2: Invest in product photography — Saudi shoppers make buying decisions based on images. A simple white background setup with good lighting outperforms even the best-written product descriptions.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s15P3: Join the Saudi Shopify community on Twitter/X and Telegram. Local sellers share app recommendations, gateway updates, and ZATCA compliance tips that you will not find in Shopify's official documentation.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13PricingDesc: All plans include a free trial. CTA buttons link to Shopify pricing.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13PricingNote: All plans include unlimited products, SSL certificate, and 24/7 support. Prices shown are in USD. Saudi merchants using third-party gateways (like Tap Payments) pay an additional transaction fee: 2.0% on Basic, 1.0% on Grow, 0.6% on Advanced.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Plan1Desc: For solo entrepreneurs. $1/month for first 3 months.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Plan1F4: In-person selling by phone or POS device
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Plan2Desc: For small teams scaling up. $1/month for first 3 months.
howToBuildShopifyStore.s13Plan3Desc: For global reach with advanced features.

shopifyVsSalla.metaTitle: Shopify vs Salla 2026: Which E-Commerce Platform Is Better for Saudi Sellers?
shopifyVsSalla.metaDescription: Detailed comparison of Shopify and Salla for Saudi Arabia — pricing, features, Arabic support, ZATCA compliance, payment gateways, shipping, and our verdict on the best platform for Saudi sellers.
shopifyVsSalla.heroImageAlt: Two monitors showing Shopify and Salla dashboards side by side in a modern Saudi office
shopifyVsSalla.heroTitle: Shopify vs Salla: Which Is Better for Saudi Sellers?
shopifyVsSalla.authorMeta: E-Commerce Analysts at Mansati | Published April 4, 2026
shopifyVsSalla.shareText: Shopify vs Salla: Which Is Better for Saudi Sellers?
shopifyVsSalla.keyTakeaway1: Salla is the better choice for Arabic-first sellers who want a fully localized experience with built-in ZATCA compliance, native Saudi payment gateways, and pricing in SAR.
shopifyVsSalla.keyTakeaway2: Shopify is the stronger platform for sellers who plan to scale internationally, need a massive app ecosystem, or want advanced customization through Liquid templating.
shopifyVsSalla.keyTakeaway3: Salla starts at 99 SAR/month (~$26) with a free plan available, while Shopify Basic costs $27/month -- making them comparable at entry level.
shopifyVsSalla.keyTakeaway4: Both platforms support Tap Payments, Tabby, Apple Pay, Aramex, and SMSA, but Salla integrates them natively while Shopify often requires third-party apps.
shopifyVsSalla.keyTakeaway5: For ZATCA Phase 2 e-invoicing compliance, Salla has it built in from day one. Shopify requires a third-party ZATCA app that costs an additional SAR 99-299/month.
shopifyVsSalla.s2Title: Why This Comparison Matters for Saudi Sellers
shopifyVsSalla.s2Lead: Saudi Arabia's e-commerce market hit $13.7 billion in 2025, growing 20% year-over-year. Whether you choose Shopify or Salla will shape your costs, compliance, and growth trajectory for years to come.
shopifyVsSalla.s2P1: If you are launching an online store in Saudi Arabia in 2026, you have two serious contenders: Shopify, the global e-commerce giant powering 4.8 million stores worldwide, and Salla, the Saudi-born platform that has rapidly grown to over 55,000 active stores across the MENA region.
shopifyVsSalla.s2P2: This is not a simple question of 'global vs local.' Each platform makes genuine trade-offs. Shopify offers unmatched scale, a 13,000+ app ecosystem, and battle-tested infrastructure. Salla offers native Arabic RTL support, built-in ZATCA compliance, and deep integration with Saudi payment and logistics providers.
shopifyVsSalla.s2P3: We tested both platforms side by side over three weeks -- setting up stores, adding products in Arabic, configuring Saudi payment gateways, testing shipping integrations, and evaluating ZATCA compliance workflows. This article covers everything we found.
shopifyVsSalla.s3Intro: Before diving into feature-by-feature comparisons, it helps to understand where each platform comes from and what it was designed to do.
shopifyVsSalla.s3ImageAlt: Saudi business owner reviewing pricing plans on a tablet with calculator and receipts on desk
shopifyVsSalla.s3ImageCaption: Evaluating the real cost differences between Shopify and Salla for Saudi e-commerce operations.
shopifyVsSalla.s3ShopifyP1: Founded in 2006 in Ottawa, Canada, Shopify is the world's largest dedicated e-commerce platform. It powers over 4.8 million online stores across 175 countries, processes over $235 billion in annual GMV, and offers everything from basic storefront hosting to enterprise-grade solutions through Shopify Plus.
shopifyVsSalla.s3ShopifyP2: For Saudi sellers, Shopify provides a solid foundation but requires additional configuration. Arabic RTL support is available through select themes, payment gateways like Tap and HyperPay must be installed as third-party apps, and ZATCA compliance requires a dedicated plugin.
shopifyVsSalla.s3SallaP1: Founded in 2016 in Riyadh, Salla (salla.com) is a Saudi-native e-commerce platform built from the ground up for the Arabic-speaking market. It now hosts over 55,000 active online stores, processes SAR 8+ billion in annual transactions, and is the fastest-growing e-commerce platform in the GCC region.
shopifyVsSalla.s3SallaP2: Salla's core advantage is localization. The entire platform -- dashboard, documentation, customer support, themes, checkout -- is Arabic-first. ZATCA Phase 2 e-invoicing is built in, Saudi payment gateways are native integrations (not plugins), and shipping with Aramex, SMSA, and J&T is preconfigured.
shopifyVsSalla.s4Title: Ease of Use: Setup, Dashboard & Arabic Support
shopifyVsSalla.s4Intro: Getting from sign-up to a functioning store is where the two platforms diverge most dramatically. The setup experience reveals the fundamental difference in their target audiences.
shopifyVsSalla.s4ShopifyP1: Shopify's onboarding is world-class -- guided setup wizards, pre-populated demo data, and helpful tooltips at every step. However, the experience is designed for English-speaking merchants first. To run a Saudi store, you will need to:
shopifyVsSalla.s4ShopifyFeature1: Switch your theme to an RTL-compatible option (not all free themes support this)
shopifyVsSalla.s4ShopifyFeature2: Install a language translation app for Arabic storefront content
shopifyVsSalla.s4ShopifyFeature3: Configure tax settings manually for Saudi 15% VAT
shopifyVsSalla.s4SallaP1: Salla's dashboard loads in Arabic by default. Every menu item, settings page, and help article is written in Arabic first. The setup wizard asks for your Saudi Commercial Registration (CR) number upfront and pre-configures VAT settings automatically.
shopifyVsSalla.s4SallaFeature1: Dashboard, checkout, and customer emails are all Arabic-first by default
shopifyVsSalla.s4SallaFeature2: CR number verification and Maroof integration are part of onboarding
shopifyVsSalla.s4SallaFeature3: 15% VAT and ZATCA e-invoicing are pre-configured -- no manual setup needed
shopifyVsSalla.s4Callout: If your primary audience is Saudi shoppers and your team operates in Arabic, Salla's setup experience saves you 2-3 hours of configuration that Shopify requires through third-party apps and manual settings.
shopifyVsSalla.s5Title: Design & Themes: Template Quality and Customization
shopifyVsSalla.s5Intro: Both platforms offer theme marketplaces, but they cater to very different design needs and skill levels.
shopifyVsSalla.s5ImageAlt: Hands using a smartphone showing an Arabic e-commerce app with product listings
shopifyVsSalla.s5ImageCaption: Mobile shopping experience -- both Shopify and Salla themes are fully responsive for Arabic RTL layouts.
shopifyVsSalla.s5ShopifyP1: Shopify offers over 200 professionally designed themes (13 free, the rest $180-$400). The Liquid templating engine gives developers full control over HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. For Saudi stores, the Dawn, Sense, and Taste themes handle RTL well, but you may still need a developer to fine-tune Arabic typography and layout details.
shopifyVsSalla.s5SallaP1: Salla's theme marketplace offers 80+ templates, all designed for Arabic RTL layouts from the start. Free themes are available, and premium themes range from SAR 200-800. While customization depth is more limited than Shopify's Liquid engine, the drag-and-drop editor handles most layout changes without code. For sellers who want a beautiful Arabic store without hiring a developer, Salla themes are ready out of the box.
shopifyVsSalla.s5P1: The trade-off is clear: Shopify wins on theme variety and deep customization for developers. Salla wins on Arabic-ready design that works without code changes.
shopifyVsSalla.s6Intro: Payment gateways and shipping carriers are where the 'local vs global' distinction matters most. Both platforms support the key Saudi providers, but the integration depth differs significantly.
shopifyVsSalla.s6P1: Salla has a clear edge in local payment and shipping integration. STC Pay, J&T Express, and RedBox lockers are Saudi-specific services that Salla supports natively but Shopify does not offer at all. For COD orders -- still 35-40% of Saudi e-commerce transactions -- Salla's built-in verification system reduces fraud rates significantly.
shopifyVsSalla.s7Intro: Both platforms offer tiered pricing, but the value proposition differs at each level. Here is how they compare head-to-head.
shopifyVsSalla.s7P1: At the entry level, both platforms cost roughly the same. But the gap widens dramatically at higher tiers. Shopify Advanced at $399/month is nearly 4x the cost of Salla Pro at 399 SAR/month (~$106). However, Shopify Advanced includes features like third-party calculated shipping rates, custom reports, and up to 15 staff accounts that Salla Pro does not match.
shopifyVsSalla.s7P2: Salla also offers a free plan (Salla Free) with limited features -- up to 50 products and basic analytics. Shopify has no free plan but offers a 3-day trial followed by $1/month for 3 months.
shopifyVsSalla.s7Callout: Hidden cost alert: Shopify charges a 2% transaction fee on non-Shopify Payments orders (reduced to 1% on the Shopify plan, 0.6% on Advanced). Since Shopify Payments is not available in Saudi Arabia, you will always pay this fee on top of your payment gateway charges. Salla does not charge any transaction fees on any plan.
shopifyVsSalla.s8Intro: Saudi Arabia's Zakat, Tax and Customs Authority (ZATCA) requires all businesses to issue Phase 2 e-invoices with QR codes and submit them electronically. This is not optional -- non-compliance results in fines starting at SAR 5,000 per violation.
shopifyVsSalla.s8ShopifyP1: Shopify does not have built-in ZATCA compliance. You need to install a ZATCA e-invoicing app from the Shopify App Store. The most popular options are:
shopifyVsSalla.s8ShopifyFeature1: ZATCA E-Invoice by Invoicemaker (SAR 149/month) -- generates QR-coded invoices and submits to ZATCA API
shopifyVsSalla.s8ShopifyFeature2: Fatora ZATCA Integration (SAR 99/month) -- basic compliance for smaller stores
shopifyVsSalla.s8ShopifyFeature3: Custom API integration through a developer (SAR 5,000-15,000 one-time setup)
shopifyVsSalla.s8SallaP1: Salla has full ZATCA Phase 2 compliance built into every plan, including the free tier. The system automatically:
shopifyVsSalla.s8SallaFeature1: Generates ZATCA-compliant e-invoices with QR codes for every transaction
shopifyVsSalla.s8SallaFeature2: Submits invoices electronically to the ZATCA portal in real time
shopifyVsSalla.s8SallaFeature3: Handles credit notes, debit notes, and invoice corrections per ZATCA specifications
shopifyVsSalla.s8Callout: For Saudi-registered businesses, ZATCA compliance is not negotiable. Salla's built-in approach saves SAR 1,188-3,588 per year compared to Shopify's app-based solution. If ZATCA compliance is your top priority, Salla is the clear winner.
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyPro1: Massive app ecosystem (13,000+ apps) for virtually any feature you need
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyPro2: Best-in-class infrastructure -- 99.99% uptime, global CDN, unlimited bandwidth
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyPro3: Deep customization through Liquid templating and headless commerce (Hydrogen)
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyPro4: Proven ability to scale from startup to enterprise (Shopify Plus)
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyPro5: Strong international selling features -- multi-currency, multi-language, global shipping
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyCon1: Arabic RTL support requires theme selection and manual configuration
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyCon2: ZATCA compliance requires a paid third-party app (SAR 99-149/month extra)
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyCon3: 2% transaction fee on all Saudi orders since Shopify Payments is not available in KSA
shopifyVsSalla.s9ShopifyCon4: Customer support is English-first with limited Arabic availability
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaPro1: Fully Arabic-native platform -- dashboard, themes, checkout, and support all in Arabic
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaPro2: Built-in ZATCA Phase 2 e-invoicing on all plans including the free tier
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaPro3: No transaction fees on any plan -- your payment gateway fees are your only cost
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaPro4: Native integration with Saudi payment gateways (STC Pay, Tabby, Tamara) and carriers (SMSA, J&T, RedBox)
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaPro5: Free plan available with up to 50 products -- lowest barrier to entry
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaCon1: Limited app ecosystem (200+ apps vs Shopify's 13,000+)
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaCon2: Theme customization is more restricted -- no equivalent to Shopify's Liquid engine
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaCon3: International selling features are basic -- primarily designed for Saudi/GCC markets
shopifyVsSalla.s9SallaCon4: Fewer third-party integrations for marketing, analytics, and CRM tools
shopifyVsSalla.s9P1: Both platforms have genuine strengths that serve different seller profiles. The 'right' choice depends entirely on your specific business needs, target market, and growth plans.
shopifyVsSalla.s10Title: Verdict: Which Platform Should You Choose?
shopifyVsSalla.s10ImageAlt: Happy Saudi entrepreneur with shipping boxes and laptop showing a successful online store
shopifyVsSalla.s10ImageCaption: The right platform choice depends on your target market, language needs, and growth plans.
shopifyVsSalla.s10ShopifyVerdictP1: Shopify is the better choice for sellers who plan to grow beyond the Saudi market or need advanced customization capabilities.
shopifyVsSalla.s10ShopifyBestFor1: You plan to sell internationally and need multi-currency, multi-language support
shopifyVsSalla.s10ShopifyBestFor2: You need advanced integrations (ERP, CRM, marketing automation) from a 13,000+ app ecosystem
shopifyVsSalla.s10ShopifyBestFor3: You have a developer or want deep theme customization through Liquid/Hydrogen
shopifyVsSalla.s10SallaVerdictP1: Salla is the better choice for Saudi-focused sellers who want a localized, low-friction experience with no hidden costs.
shopifyVsSalla.s10SallaBestFor1: Your primary market is Saudi Arabia and your team operates in Arabic
shopifyVsSalla.s10SallaBestFor2: ZATCA compliance, STC Pay, and local shipping carriers are must-haves from day one
shopifyVsSalla.s10SallaBestFor3: You want the lowest total cost of ownership with no transaction fees and SAR pricing
shopifyVsSalla.s10P1: For most Saudi-focused sellers launching their first online store, Salla offers the faster path to revenue. The Arabic-native experience, built-in ZATCA compliance, native payment gateways, and zero transaction fees create a compelling package that Shopify cannot match without significant additional configuration and cost.
shopifyVsSalla.s10P2: For sellers with international ambitions, a development team, or complex integration requirements, Shopify remains the more powerful and flexible platform. Its ecosystem depth and proven scalability are unmatched in the e-commerce space.
shopifyVsSalla.s11Q1: Can I use both Shopify and Salla at the same time?
shopifyVsSalla.s11A1: Technically yes, but it is not recommended. Managing inventory, orders, and customer data across two platforms creates operational complexity. Most sellers pick one platform and commit. If you are unsure, start with Salla's free plan to test the local experience, then compare with Shopify's $1/month trial period.
shopifyVsSalla.s11Q2: Is Salla only available in Arabic?
shopifyVsSalla.s11A2: No. Salla supports both Arabic and English for storefronts. However, the admin dashboard and primary documentation are Arabic-first. If your team operates primarily in English, Shopify's interface may feel more natural.
shopifyVsSalla.s11Q3: Does Shopify support Saudi Riyal (SAR) as a currency?
shopifyVsSalla.s11A3: Yes. Shopify supports SAR as a store currency. However, since Shopify Payments is not available in Saudi Arabia, you will use a third-party payment gateway like Tap Payments and pay Shopify's 2% transaction fee on top of gateway charges.
shopifyVsSalla.s11Q4: Which platform is better for dropshipping in Saudi Arabia?
shopifyVsSalla.s11A4: Shopify has a stronger dropshipping ecosystem with apps like DSers, Spocket, and CJdropshipping. Salla has fewer dropshipping integrations but supports local dropshipping workflows with Saudi suppliers. If dropshipping is your primary model, Shopify is the better choice.
shopifyVsSalla.s11Q5: Can I migrate from Salla to Shopify (or vice versa)?
shopifyVsSalla.s11A5: Yes, both platforms support data import/export. Salla offers a migration tool for moving to their platform, and Shopify has CSV import capabilities for products, customers, and orders. Expect 2-4 hours of work for a store with under 500 products. For larger catalogs, consider hiring a migration specialist.
